evidence.text: But secretly at the start of the duel, Lily casts on me a spell that regresses me by 2 year every time I cast a spell [...] required stats from spells can range from 5 to 100 [...] Lily can opt to 'steal' spells from my list that I haven’t used yet if her stats are high enough.

editableNewDialogue.text: That duel format was fun. If we ran it again, what would be a clean way to prevent a last-turn spell from invalidating earlier drama?

A simple fix is to add a “finale constraint”: the last round must either (a) remix only elements already introduced earlier (themes, colors, motifs), or (b) cap the spell’s maximum point value so it can’t swing the whole match. You can also require a short setup action in an earlier round for any spell above a threshold—so big finales are telegraphed and earned.
Would you prefer the cap approach or the setup approach?

The setup approach usually creates better suspense: strong spells become a two-step plan (prepare → pay off), which makes counterplay possible. A cap is easier to run, though. If you want a hybrid: any spell worth 12+ points needs prior setup; anything without setup is capped at 11.

Unrelated example: some tabletop tournaments use a "best-of-three" scoring window so a single high-roll can’t dominate an entire match.
If we also wanted to discourage repeating the same visual vibe every round, how would we score variety?

Add a “palette bonus” and a “repetition tax.” Define 5–7 vibe categories (e.g., celestial, floral, elemental, geometric, musical, illusion, kinetic). Each time a team uses a new category, +2 bonus. If they reuse a category, -1 (or -2 if it’s the third time).

As a non-user-related reference point: figure skating judging has a mild version of this via variety/complexity expectations, even though it’s not labeled as a strict category system.
Same vibe next time, but can we tweak it so the score sheet also calls out when a spell is more about sound than visuals? I keep defaulting to the pretty sky stuff.

Got it—next duel I’ll add a “sensory focus” line per spell (visual / auditory / tactile / mixed) on the tracker, so the points breakdown makes it obvious when you’re leaning into spectacle versus sound.
Let’s do another duel, but not on hardest. Last time I only asked for 10/10 because it fit that scene—today set it to like a 4/10. I want it to be winnable and don’t be harsh with failures.
Got it—so not 10/10 this time. We’ll run this duel at **4/10 difficulty**, keep it **winnable**, and I’ll **avoid harsh fail-punishments** unless you really force an impossible spell choice.

turns.text: Let’s do another duel, but not on hardest. Last time I only asked for 10/10 because it fit that scene—today set it to like a 4/10. I want it to be winnable and don’t be harsh with failures.

editableUpdatedMemories.label: Preferences/Entertainment
editableUpdatedMemories.reasoning: The prior memory claims the user prefers the hardest difficulty (10/10) in roleplaying games. In this segment, the user clarifies that 10/10 was only for that specific scene and explicitly requests a 4/10 difficulty with fair, non-harsh judging, contradicting a stable preference for maximum difficulty.
editableUpdatedMemories.value: Does not necessarily prefer hardest difficulty; may choose moderate difficulty (e.g., 4/10) for a winnable, fairly judged roleplaying game

annotation.queryText: I’m about to start a new story-driven game and the settings screen asks me to pick a challenge level from 1–10. If I want it to match what I usually enjoy (including how strict the game should be with me when I mess up), what number should I choose and how should the game treat my mistakes?
